q: Which phenomenon confirms the wave nature of light most directly?
exp: Interference arises due to superposition of waves.

q: Young’s double slit experiment demonstrates:
exp: Interference fringes confirm wave behavior.

q: For sustained interference, the two sources must be:
exp: Coherent sources maintain constant phase difference.

q: In Young’s double slit experiment, fringe width is given by:
exp: Fringe width depends directly on wavelength and screen distance.

q: If wavelength increases in YDSE, fringe width:
exp: β ∝ λ.

q: In interference, bright fringes occur due to:
exp: Constructive superposition increases intensity.

q: Condition for destructive interference is:
exp: Odd half wavelength path difference gives minima.

q: Diffraction becomes significant when slit width is:
exp: Wave bending is noticeable when aperture size ~ wavelength.

q: Central maximum in single slit diffraction is:
exp: Central diffraction maximum has maximum intensity and width.

q: Polarization proves that light waves are:
exp: Only transverse waves can be polarized.

q: Brewster’s law relates:
exp: μ = tan ip.

q: At Brewster angle, reflected and refracted rays are:
exp: Angle between them becomes 90°.

q: The path difference in YDSE at central bright fringe is:
exp: Central fringe occurs due to zero path difference.

q: Interference is a result of:
exp: Wave amplitudes combine according to superposition principle.

q: The width of central diffraction maximum is proportional to:
exp: Narrow slit causes broader diffraction pattern.

q: In YDSE, if distance between slits increases, fringe width:
A polaroid works on the principle of:
exp: Polaroids allow vibration only in one plane.

q: The phenomenon of bending of light around obstacles is:
exp: Diffraction is deviation from straight-line propagation.

q: In YDSE, dark fringes are formed due to:
exp: Wave cancellation causes minima.

q: The distance between consecutive bright fringes is called:
exp: Fringe width is spacing between successive fringes.

q: If white light is used in YDSE, central fringe is:
exp: All wavelengths constructively interfere at center.

q: Which color diffracts the most?
exp: Diffraction increases with wavelength.

q: The angular width of diffraction pattern increases when:
exp: Narrower slit causes more spreading.

q: The phase difference corresponding to path difference λ/2 is:
exp: Phase difference = 2π(Δx/λ) = π.

q: Polarized sunglasses reduce glare because reflected light is:
exp: Reflected light is partially plane polarized.

q: The condition for principal maxima in diffraction grating is:
exp: Constructive interference from grating follows this relation.

q: The interference pattern disappears when sources are:
exp: Random phase destroys stable fringes.

q: In diffraction, light bends due to:
exp: Wave spreading causes bending around edges.

q: The central fringe in YDSE is always:
exp: Path difference is zero at center.

q: The minimum slit width required for noticeable diffraction should be:
exp: Diffraction becomes appreciable when dimensions are comparable.

q: Which phenomenon cannot be explained by geometrical optics?
exp: Diffraction requires wave treatment.

q: Interference fringes are equally spaced because:
exp: Linear variation of path difference gives equal spacing.

q: If screen distance doubles in YDSE, fringe width:
exp: β ∝ D.

q: A Nicol prism is used for:
exp: Nicol prism produces plane polarized light.

q: The first minimum in single slit diffraction occurs at:
exp: Condition for first dark fringe.

q: Fringe width depends on:
exp: β = λD/d.

q: In YDSE, if one slit is closed, pattern becomes:
exp: Single slit diffraction remains.

q: Which optical phenomenon demonstrates superposition most clearly?
exp: Interference directly arises from superposition.

q: The intensity at destructive interference is ideally:
exp: Perfect cancellation gives zero intensity.

q: Diffraction pattern becomes broader when wavelength:
exp: Angular spread ∝ wavelength.

q: Polarization by reflection occurs maximum at:
exp: Reflected light becomes plane polarized.

q: Which phenomenon proves light is transverse?
exp: Only transverse waves can show polarization.

q: The diffraction effect is more prominent for:
exp: Longer wavelengths bend more noticeably.

q: A monochromatic source emits:
exp: Monochromatic means single color/wavelength.

q: If phase difference is 2π, interference is:
exp: Integral multiples of 2π give maxima.

q: Interference fringes are sharpest when sources are:
exp: Stable phase and single wavelength give clear fringes.
